name: Flying
rules_text_patterns: this creature can't be blocked except by creatures with flying or reach
false_positive_notes: Flying alone is not a deck theme unless the commander rewards flying creatures, evasive attackers, or combat damage.
reason_language: helps the commander or key attackers connect in combat
strategy_language: Use evasion when the commander needs to attack or deal combat damage to generate value.

name: Reach
rules_text_patterns: a creature with reach can block creatures with flying
false_positive_notes: Reach is purely defensive support. Almost never a deck theme; it just plugs the flying-blocker hole.
reason_language: lets ground creatures block the table's flyers
strategy_language: Reach matters when opposing flying decks would otherwise punch through unblocked.

name: Trample
rules_text_patterns: this creature can deal excess combat damage to the player or planeswalker it's attacking
false_positive_notes: Trample alone is not a theme. It is a damage-throughput tool that matters when the deck buffs creatures large or stacks +1/+1 counters.
reason_language: pushes through chump blockers when attackers are oversized
strategy_language: Trample shines on big creatures, voltron commanders, and +1/+1 counter decks where one attacker carries combat.

name: Menace
rules_text_patterns: this creature can't be blocked except by two or more creatures
false_positive_notes: Menace is light evasion, not a theme. It helps swarm and goblin decks push damage but rarely defines a deck.
reason_language: makes attackers harder to block one-for-one
strategy_language: Menace pairs well with token decks and combat-damage triggers.

name: Vigilance
rules_text_patterns: attacking doesn't cause this creature to tap
false_positive_notes: Vigilance is defensive value. Some commanders that tap for mana or trigger on attack want it, but it is not a deck theme.
reason_language: lets attackers stay back to block on the way out
strategy_language: Vigilance matters most for commanders that tap or are repeated targets after combat.

name: Haste
rules_text_patterns: this creature can attack and {T} as soon as it comes under your control
false_positive_notes: Haste is a tempo enabler, not a theme. Reanimator, blink, and token decks especially appreciate it.
reason_language: lets new creatures attack or tap immediately
strategy_language: Haste matters when a creature must immediately attack or activate, like for reanimator finishers and combat-trigger commanders.

name: First Strike
rules_text_patterns: this creature deals combat damage before creatures without first strike
false_positive_notes: First strike is a combat tool. It rarely defines a deck unless paired with deathtouch or combat-damage triggers.
reason_language: wins combat before opposing creatures can swing back
strategy_language: First strike is best with deathtouch or with combat-damage triggers that should resolve first.

name: Double Strike
rules_text_patterns: this creature deals both first-strike and regular combat damage
false_positive_notes: Double strike doubles attacks and triggers combat damage twice. Strong on voltron and on commander damage plans, but not a theme alone.
reason_language: deals combat damage twice and stacks combat-damage triggers
strategy_language: Double strike is a force multiplier on voltron and on cards that trigger when a creature deals combat damage.

name: Deathtouch
rules_text_patterns: any amount of damage this deals to a creature is enough to destroy it
false_positive_notes: Deathtouch becomes a deck theme only with payoffs (pingers, trample carriers, deathtouch tribal). Otherwise it is generic protection.
reason_language: turns any damage from this creature into removal
strategy_language: Deathtouch is best with trample, pingers, and fight effects that turn one damage into a kill.

name: Lifelink
rules_text_patterns: damage dealt by this creature also causes you to gain that much life
false_positive_notes: Lifelink alone is not a theme. It enables lifegain decks and stabilizes aggressive plans, but needs lifegain payoffs to matter as a strategy.
reason_language: converts combat damage into life that can fuel lifegain payoffs
strategy_language: Lifelink is a theme enabler — pair with lifegain triggers like Trelasarra or Karlov for it to matter beyond stabilization.

name: Hexproof
rules_text_patterns: this creature can't be the target of spells or abilities your opponents control
false_positive_notes: Hexproof is protection. It defines voltron and aura-based plans where the hero must survive, but is otherwise support.
reason_language: shields the carrier from opposing targeted removal
strategy_language: Hexproof is most valuable on voltron and aura-stack commanders that lose if the centerpiece dies.

name: Shroud
rules_text_patterns: this can't be the target of spells or abilities
false_positive_notes: Shroud also blocks your own targeting, which often hurts auras and equipment plans. Older, less common than hexproof.
reason_language: makes the carrier untargetable, including by allies
strategy_language: Shroud is a double-edged sword — it stops opponents but also blocks your buff spells. Prefer hexproof when possible.

name: Ward
rules_text_patterns: whenever this becomes the target of a spell or ability an opponent controls, counter it unless that player pays
false_positive_notes: Ward is soft protection. Strong on commanders, but rarely a deck-defining theme.
reason_language: taxes opposing removal aimed at the carrier
strategy_language: Ward is a tempo wall, not absolute protection. Layer it with redundancy on must-resolve threats.

name: Indestructible
rules_text_patterns: damage and effects that say 'destroy' don't destroy this
false_positive_notes: Indestructible blanks board wipes and damage but not exile, sacrifice, or -X/-X. Best on key permanents and commanders.
reason_language: ignores damage-based and destruction-based removal
strategy_language: Pair indestructible with sweepers — Avacyn-style 'wrath but I survive' plans are powerful.

name: Defender
rules_text_patterns: this creature can't attack
false_positive_notes: Defender becomes a theme only with walls-matter payoffs like Arcades or Doran. Most defenders are pure blockers otherwise.
reason_language: fills the board with toughness-heavy bodies that walls payoffs reward
strategy_language: Defender is a deck theme only with Arcades, Doran, or toughness-matters payoffs that make walls attack.

name: Flash
rules_text_patterns: you may cast this spell any time you could cast an instant
false_positive_notes: Flash is theme-defining only with flash-matters commanders or archetypes. Otherwise it is responsive texture.
reason_language: deploys threats and blockers at instant speed for ambush plays
strategy_language: Flash is a real theme with commanders like Yeva or Vivien that turn the deck into a permission/ambush list.

name: Protection
rules_text_patterns: damage that would be dealt to this by sources of the chosen color is prevented
false_positive_notes: Protection is multi-purpose: evasion, removal-proofing, and aura/equipment hate. Theme only with mother of runes-style payoffs.
reason_language: evades blockers and removal in the chosen color
strategy_language: Protection is one of the strongest combat words; on voltron it is often better than hexproof because it also avoids being blocked.

name: Enchant
rules_text_patterns: enchant
false_positive_notes: Enchant is a card-type rule on auras. Aura strategies are themes; the keyword itself is just identification.
reason_language: marks the card as an aura that attaches to the chosen permanent type
strategy_language: Enchant identifies auras; the strategy comes from aura-matters commanders, not the keyword itself.

name: Equip
rules_text_patterns: equip {cost}
attach this equipment to target creature you control
false_positive_notes: Equip identifies equipment. Equipment-matters and voltron commanders make it a real theme; otherwise it is texture.
reason_language: attaches gear to creatures, fueling voltron and equipment-tribal payoffs
strategy_language: Equip becomes a theme with commanders that cheat equip costs (Sram, Nahiri, Kemba) or reward equipment count.

name: Scry
rules_text_patterns: look at the top N cards of your library, then put any number of them on the bottom and the rest on top in any order
false_positive_notes: Scry is generic card selection. It becomes a theme only with scry-matters or top-of-library commanders.
reason_language: smooths draws and sets up top-of-library plays
strategy_language: Scry shines with miracle, top-of-library reveal commanders, or constellation-style draw chains.

name: Surveil
rules_text_patterns: look at the top N cards of your library. then you may put any number of them into your graveyard and the rest on top of your library in any order
false_positive_notes: Surveil is graveyard fuel and card filtering. Theme strength depends on graveyard payoffs and surveil-matters payoffs.
reason_language: filters draws while also stocking the graveyard
strategy_language: Surveil is strongest in decks that want both graveyard density and selection — reanimator-lite, escape, and delve plans.

name: Mill
rules_text_patterns: mill N cards
put the top N cards of your library into your graveyard
false_positive_notes: Mill can be self-mill (fuel for graveyard plans) or opponent-mill (decking as a win condition). Identify which the deck is doing before treating it as a theme.
requires: graveyard payoff or library size payoff
reason_language: fills the graveyard for recursion or attacks an opponent's library
strategy_language: Self-mill wants graveyard payoffs; opponent-mill wants either huge mill-per-turn or graveyard hate to keep their yard small.

name: Cycling
rules_text_patterns: cycling {cost}
discard this card: draw a card
false_positive_notes: Cycling is both card-smoothing texture and a true theme with cycling-matters commanders. Distinguish 'has cycling' from 'cycling matters'.
requires: cycling payoff or pure card flow goals
reason_language: discards a card to draw a card, fueling cycling-matters payoffs and graveyard plans
strategy_language: Cycling becomes a deck theme with payoffs like Astral Drift, Drannith Stinger, or Lonis style triggers; otherwise it is just smoothing.

name: Fight
rules_text_patterns: each deals damage equal to its power to the other
false_positive_notes: Fight is removal-on-a-creature. Most decks treat it as targeted removal, not a theme. Becomes spicy with deathtouch and big-creature decks.
reason_language: removes opposing creatures using your own creatures' power
strategy_language: Pair fight with deathtouch to kill anything, or with big creatures and counters to make it real removal.

name: Goad
rules_text_patterns: that creature attacks each combat if able and attacks a player other than you
false_positive_notes: Goad needs repeatable goad sources to be a theme; one or two goad cards is just texture.
reason_language: redirects opposing creatures away from you and at each other
strategy_language: Goad needs a defensive board to survive — without blockers, goad just speeds up your loss.

name: Sacrifice
oracle_patterns: whenever a creature you control dies
rules_text_patterns: sacrifice
false_positive_notes: Sacrifice as an additional cost on individual cards is texture. The theme requires both sac outlets and sac payoffs (death triggers, drain, scaling).
requires: death trigger payoff or sacrifice payoff
reason_language: feeds outlets that turn dying creatures into damage, draw, or value
strategy_language: An aristocrats deck needs three pieces: a free sac outlet, a payoff that triggers on death or sacrifice, and a steady supply of fodder.

name: Exile
rules_text_patterns: exile
false_positive_notes: Exile appears on most removal spells; that alone is texture. Exile-matters themes need cast-from-exile or exile-trigger payoffs.
requires: cast-from-exile payoff or exile trigger payoff
reason_language: moves cards to exile to be cast later or to power exile-matters payoffs
strategy_language: Exile-matters needs payoffs like Prosper or Ragavan that reward casting cards from exile or that trigger on exile-cast.

name: Discard
rules_text_patterns: discard
false_positive_notes: Discard splits into two themes: opponent-discard (hand attack) and self-discard (loot/madness/reanimator). They need different payoffs.
reason_language: feeds graveyard payoffs or attacks opposing hands
strategy_language: Self-discard themes want madness, flashback, escape, or reanimator payoffs. Opponent-discard themes want repeatable triggered hand attack and lock pieces.

name: Counter (spell)
rules_text_patterns: counter target spell
false_positive_notes: Counterspells are individually interaction; the theme is control. Distinguish a few permission cards from a real counter-tribal/counters-matter plan.
reason_language: stops opposing spells before they resolve
strategy_language: Counterspells protect engines and stop wins. Specific counter-cast payoffs (Talrand, Baral, Jeskai Ascendancy) make counters a theme.

name: Destroy
rules_text_patterns: destroy
false_positive_notes: Destroy is the most generic removal verb. It is interaction texture, not a theme.
reason_language: removes opposing permanents
strategy_language: Destroy fails against indestructible; pair with exile or sacrifice removal where needed.

name: Tap
rules_text_patterns: tap
false_positive_notes: Tap is everywhere. The theme is 'tap matters' — pillowfort/lockdown decks that prevent untap or punish tapped creatures.
reason_language: locks opposing permanents or fuels tap-matters payoffs
strategy_language: Tap themes want repeatable tap effects and don't-untap riders, plus mana to keep doing it (Derevi-style).

name: Untap
rules_text_patterns: untap
false_positive_notes: Untap is a combo enabler with mana dorks and tap-abilities. It is also pillowfort relief. Identify which role applies.
reason_language: reuses tap abilities and mana producers within a single turn
strategy_language: Untap effects pair with creatures that produce more mana than they cost to tap, enabling fast combos and Craterhoof-style finishers.

name: Create (token)
rules_text_patterns: create
false_positive_notes: Token creation is core to many decks. The detector should distinguish creature tokens (go-wide) from artifact tokens (treasure/food/clue) since payoffs differ.
reason_language: produces bodies that fuel anthems, sacrifice outlets, and combat
strategy_language: Token decks want anthems, doublers (Doubling Season, Parallel Lives), and protection from sweepers.

name: Search (library)
rules_text_patterns: search your library for
false_positive_notes: Library search is consistency. Tutors are powerful but not a theme; land tutors are ramp; tutor-tribal is rare.
reason_language: fetches the exact card the deck needs from the library
strategy_language: Tutors are consistency, not a theme. Identify whether searches are land-tutor (ramp) or card-tutor (combo/toolbox).

name: Reveal
rules_text_patterns: reveal
false_positive_notes: Reveal is procedural. It matters when paired with top-of-library matters or with cipher/cascade/discover-like cast-from-exile chains.
reason_language: shows information and powers reveal-trigger payoffs
strategy_language: Reveal is mostly texture; it becomes a theme only with top-of-library payoffs like Maelstrom Wanderer or Magus of the Future.

name: Investigate
rules_text_patterns: create a colorless clue artifact token with
false_positive_notes: Investigate is card draw via clues. Theme strength depends on clue/artifact payoffs and sacrifice synergies.
requires: artifact payoff or card draw goal
reason_language: creates clues that smooth draws and feed artifact payoffs
strategy_language: Investigate becomes a theme with clue-matters or artifact sacrifice payoffs; otherwise it is just card flow.

name: Populate
rules_text_patterns: create a token that's a copy of a creature token you control
false_positive_notes: Populate needs an existing token of value to copy. It is a theme with big-token plans, not generic token decks.
requires: large or valuable creature token in play
reason_language: copies your best existing creature token for free
strategy_language: Populate wants big single tokens to copy (angels, dragons), not small swarms of 1/1s.

name: Manifest
rules_text_patterns: put the top card of your library onto the battlefield face down as a 2/2 creature
false_positive_notes: Manifest matters when the deck stacks the top of library, has cheap flip costs, or pairs with morph payoffs.
reason_language: puts cards onto the battlefield face down for cheap mana and surprise flips
strategy_language: Manifest is best with top-of-library manipulation and morph synergy commanders.

name: Cloak
rules_text_patterns: put a card from your hand onto the battlefield face down as a 2/2 creature with ward
false_positive_notes: Cloak is manifest-from-hand with ward. It plays as a manifest variant and wants the same flip and morph payoffs.
reason_language: deploys hidden 2/2s with ward that can flip into bigger threats
strategy_language: Cloak shines with disguise and morph synergies and with face-down-matters payoffs.

name: Manifest Dread
rules_text_patterns: look at the top two cards of your library, manifest one of them, then put the other into your graveyard
false_positive_notes: Manifest dread filters and stocks the graveyard. It supports manifest, morph, and self-mill plans.
reason_language: filters the top of the library while feeding graveyard plans and face-down creatures
strategy_language: Manifest dread is strong in mill-and-flip decks; combine with morph and graveyard payoffs.

name: Bolster
rules_text_patterns: choose a creature with the least toughness among creatures you control. put a +1/+1 counter on it
false_positive_notes: Bolster is a +1/+1 counter source. Theme strength depends on the rest of the +1/+1 counter package.
reason_language: places +1/+1 counters on the smallest creature, fueling counter strategies
strategy_language: Bolster is a counters subtheme; treat as a counter producer and judge in context.

name: Detain
rules_text_patterns: until your next turn, that permanent can't attack or block and its activated abilities can't be activated
false_positive_notes: Detain is one-shot lockdown. Theme only with detain-matters or repeatable detain commanders.
reason_language: shuts down opposing permanents temporarily without removing them
strategy_language: Detain is interaction texture; it becomes a theme with Lavinia or Tolsimir-style payoffs.

name: Explore
rules_text_patterns: reveal the top card of your library. if a land card is revealed, put it into your hand. otherwise, put a +1/+1 counter on this creature, then put the card back or into your graveyard
false_positive_notes: Explore is hybrid: card draw, ramp, +1/+1 counters, and self-mill all in one. Theme strength depends on which aspect the deck pays off.
requires: counter payoff or graveyard or land payoff
reason_language: draws lands, scales creatures, and stocks the graveyard with one trigger
strategy_language: Explore commanders like Pantlaza or Wildgrowth Walker turn this into a real engine; otherwise treat as flexible value.

name: Learn
rules_text_patterns: reveal a lesson card you own from outside the game and put it into your hand, or discard a card to draw a card
false_positive_notes: In Commander, lessons from outside the game are not allowed; learn becomes a discard-and-draw effect. Theme value drops accordingly.
reason_language: filters a card or fetches a Lesson sideboard card in formats that allow it
strategy_language: In Commander, treat learn as rummage; only matters as part of magecraft or Strixhaven plans.

name: Monstrosity
rules_text_patterns: if this isn't monstrous, put N +1/+1 counters on it and it becomes monstrous
false_positive_notes: Monstrosity is a card-by-card mana sink with one-time +1/+1 counter placement. Rarely a theme; supports counter and mana-sink decks.
reason_language: uses spare mana to grow creatures with counters
strategy_language: Monstrosity is a mana sink with counters; treat as counters texture.

name: Regenerate
rules_text_patterns: the next time this creature would be destroyed this turn, instead remove all damage from it, tap it, and remove it from combat
false_positive_notes: Regenerate is mostly retired wording. It is light protection against damage and destroy.
reason_language: saves a creature from destruction once per shield
strategy_language: Regenerate is a niche protection word that does not stop exile or sacrifice.

name: Conjure
rules_text_patterns: conjure a card
false_positive_notes: Conjure is an Arena/Alchemy-only mechanic. It does not exist in paper Commander and should be ignored unless the format explicitly allows digital cards.
reason_language: creates a card from outside the game in digital play
strategy_language: Conjure cards are digital-only; ignore for tabletop Commander analysis.

name: Time Travel
rules_text_patterns: for each suspended card you own and each permanent you control with a time counter, you may add or remove a time counter
false_positive_notes: Time travel only matters with suspend, vanishing, and time-counter cards in the deck.
reason_language: advances or rewinds time counters on suspend and vanishing cards
strategy_language: Time travel is the heart of suspend-tribal; without suspend payoffs, it is dead text.

name: Venture into the Dungeon
rules_text_patterns: choose a dungeon and enter it, or advance to the next room
false_positive_notes: Venture pays off with repeated triggers. One or two venture cards is texture; the theme needs venture-matters payoffs.
reason_language: enters and advances dungeons for incremental triggers and a completion payoff
strategy_language: Venture decks want repeatable venture and dungeon-completion payoffs to scale.

name: Roll Dice
rules_text_patterns: roll a d20
false_positive_notes: Dice rolling is a real theme with payoffs (Pixie Guide, Wyll). Without payoffs, it is variance texture.
reason_language: rolls dice that scale with reroll and roll-matters payoffs
strategy_language: Dice decks want both rerolls and roll-trigger payoffs to convert variance into reliable value.

name: Saddle
rules_text_patterns: tap any number of other untapped creatures you control with total power N or more
false_positive_notes: Saddle requires untapped creatures with power. Theme value depends on Mount payoffs and on having cheap power-makers like tokens.
reason_language: activates Mounts by tapping enough creatures, like crew for vehicles
strategy_language: Saddle decks want token producers and untap effects to keep Mounts active.

name: Crew
rules_text_patterns: tap any number of creatures you control with total power N or more: this vehicle becomes an artifact creature until end of turn
false_positive_notes: Crew identifies Vehicles. Theme value comes from Vehicle-matters commanders and from cheap power-providers.
reason_language: turns Vehicles into creatures by tapping crew
strategy_language: Vehicle decks want token producers, mana dorks, and crew-matters payoffs to make crewing free or repeatable.

name: Proliferate
rules_text_patterns: choose any number of permanents and/or players, then give each another counter of a kind already there
false_positive_notes: Proliferate is strong only when the deck reliably places meaningful counters first. Without counter producers, it does nothing.
reason_language: multiplies counters after the commander establishes them
strategy_language: Make sure the deck places counters before adding proliferate payoffs; proliferate is a multiplier, not an engine.

name: +1/+1 Counters
rules_text_patterns: +1/+1 counter
false_positive_notes: Detect both 'gives +1/+1 counters' and 'rewards creatures with counters'. Many decks place counters but lack payoffs.
reason_language: stacks +1/+1 counters that scale creatures and trigger counters-matters payoffs
strategy_language: Counters decks need both placement (Hardened Scales effects) and payoffs (Walking Ballista, Inspiring Call).

name: -1/-1 Counters
rules_text_patterns: -1/-1 counter
false_positive_notes: -1/-1 counters appear on persist and wither cards too. Distinguish 'I put -1/-1 counters on opponents' from 'I have -1/-1 counters and want to remove or proliferate them'.
reason_language: shrinks opposing creatures with -1/-1 counters and scales triggers off them
strategy_language: -1/-1 counter decks like Hapatra want both placement and triggers off counter placement (snake tokens, drain).

name: Loyalty Counters
rules_text_patterns: loyalty
false_positive_notes: Loyalty counters define Superfriends/planeswalker decks. Detect via planeswalker count and loyalty-counter payoffs.
reason_language: drives planeswalker abilities and superfriends payoffs
strategy_language: Superfriends needs blockers, proliferate, and protection — planeswalkers die fast without defense.

name: Charge Counters
rules_text_patterns: charge counter
false_positive_notes: Charge counters appear on artifacts and lands; theme value depends on charge-matters payoffs and proliferate.
reason_language: feeds charge-counter artifacts and proliferate payoffs
strategy_language: Charge counters work in artifact-counters decks; proliferate amplifies them.

name: Poison Counters
rules_text_patterns: poison counter
false_positive_notes: Poison is a win condition (10 counters in Commander). Without infect or toxic redundancy, it is not a credible plan.
reason_language: advances opponents to ten poison counters as an alternate win condition
strategy_language: Poison decks need infect and toxic creatures, evasion, pump, and proliferate to close.

name: Energy Counters
rules_text_patterns: energy counter
false_positive_notes: Energy is a Kaladesh-block resource that mostly stays in Standard but has Commander payoffs. Theme strength depends on density.
reason_language: produces and spends energy as a parallel resource
strategy_language: Energy decks need both producers (Aetherworks) and spenders (Aethersphere Harvester) to function.

name: Experience Counters
rules_text_patterns: experience counter
false_positive_notes: Experience counters are commander-specific resources. Only relevant when the commander itself uses them.
reason_language: tracks the commander's experience to scale up effects
strategy_language: Experience commanders want fast counter accumulation and protection — losing the commander does not lose experience, but stalling does.

name: Lore Counters
rules_text_patterns: sagas
false_positive_notes: Lore counters drive Saga progression. Theme value depends on Saga density and saga-matters payoffs.
reason_language: advances sagas through their chapters and triggers lore-matters payoffs
strategy_language: Saga decks like proliferate (to skip ahead), blink (to restart), and ways to keep sagas around past the final chapter.

name: Treasure
rules_text_patterns: {T}, sacrifice this artifact: add one mana of any color
false_positive_notes: Treasure production is a core theme only when the commander rewards artifacts, artifact sacrifice, token creation, or mana bursts. Otherwise it is generic ramp.
reason_language: creates spendable artifact tokens that can become mana, sacrifice fodder, or artifact-count material
strategy_language: Treasures should feed a payoff, sacrifice line, or mana sink instead of being treated as generic ramp only.

name: Food
rules_text_patterns: {2}, {T}, sacrifice this artifact: you gain 3 life
false_positive_notes: Food is lifegain on a stick and artifact-sacrifice fodder. Theme strength depends on food-matters and lifegain payoffs.
reason_language: creates artifact tokens that gain life and feed artifact sacrifice
strategy_language: Food themes pair lifegain payoffs with artifact-matters cards; without payoffs, food is incremental life.

name: Clue
rules_text_patterns: {2}, sacrifice this artifact: draw a card
false_positive_notes: Clues are card draw on a stick. Theme value comes from artifact and clue-matters payoffs, not from drawing alone.
requires: artifact payoff or card flow goal
reason_language: creates artifact tokens that can be cracked for cards and feed artifact strategies
strategy_language: Clue decks want investigate redundancy and artifact payoffs that turn clues into more than just draws.

name: Blood
rules_text_patterns: {1}, {T}, discard a card, sacrifice this artifact: draw a card
false_positive_notes: Blood is a discard outlet plus card draw. Theme value comes from discard and madness payoffs.
reason_language: creates artifact tokens that loot away cards while fueling discard themes
strategy_language: Blood is best in vampire decks, madness decks, and discard-matters lists.

name: Powerstone
rules_text_patterns: {T}: add {C}. this mana can't be spent to cast a nonartifact spell
false_positive_notes: Powerstones are restricted ramp (artifact-spell only). Useful in artifact decks and big-creature/noncreature plans.
requires: artifact spells or noncreature artifact payoff
reason_language: ramps into artifact spells, especially big finishers like Eldrazi
strategy_language: Powerstones are not generic mana — plan around their restriction with artifact and creature spells.

name: Map
rules_text_patterns: {1}, {T}, sacrifice this artifact: target creature you control explores
false_positive_notes: Maps are explore-on-a-stick. Theme value comes from explore-matters and artifact payoffs.
reason_language: creates artifact tokens that grant explore triggers when sacrificed
strategy_language: Map decks want explore payoffs and artifact synergy; maps double as ramp via lands found.

name: Junk
rules_text_patterns: {2}, {T}, sacrifice this artifact: exile the top card of your library. you may play that card this turn
false_positive_notes: Junk tokens give impulse draw. Theme value comes from impulse-draw and artifact payoffs.
reason_language: creates artifact tokens that exile-and-cast the top of the library
strategy_language: Junk works with cast-from-exile payoffs (Prosper) and artifact themes.

name: Gold
rules_text_patterns: sacrifice this artifact: add one mana of any color
false_positive_notes: Gold tokens are pre-Treasure ramp. Almost identical role; treat as Treasure-equivalent for theme purposes.
reason_language: creates one-shot mana artifact tokens, like older Treasures
strategy_language: Gold plays like Treasure in artifact and ramp decks.

name: Servo
rules_text_patterns: 1/1 colorless servo artifact creature token
false_positive_notes: Servos are colorless 1/1 artifact creatures. Theme value with artifact and tokens-matter strategies.
requires: token payoff or artifact creature payoff
reason_language: creates 1/1 colorless artifact creatures that double as tokens and artifacts
strategy_language: Servos work in artifact-creature plans and double-token strategies.

name: Thopter
rules_text_patterns: 1/1 colorless thopter artifact creature token with flying
false_positive_notes: Thopters are flying artifact creatures. They double-count for both artifact and tokens themes.
requires: token payoff or artifact creature payoff
reason_language: creates 1/1 flying artifact creatures that count for both artifact and token payoffs
strategy_language: Thopters are stronger than Servos for combat damage thanks to flying; they shine in Sword of the Animist-style equipment plans.

name: Adapt
rules_text_patterns: if this creature has no +1/+1 counters on it, put N +1/+1 counters on it
false_positive_notes: Adapt is a one-shot per creature. It supports +1/+1 counter themes but rarely defines a deck alone.
reason_language: places +1/+1 counters via a one-time mana investment
strategy_language: Adapt is counters texture; treat as a counter source and judge in context.

name: Addendum
rules_text_patterns: if you cast this spell during your main phase
false_positive_notes: Addendum is a small bonus on instants cast in main phase. Texture only.
reason_language: rewards main-phase casting of an instant with a bonus effect

strategy_language: Transmute is a great combo finder (Drift of Phantasms for Pact of Negation, etc.).

name: Undying

false_positive_notes: Undying combos with sacrifice and counter removal (Mikaeus, Hardened Scales); supports aristocrats but isn't a theme without payoffs.

strategy_language: Undying combos with Mikaeus, the Unhallowed and counter doublers for infinite loops.

name: Unearth
rules_text_patterns: pay this cost: return this card from your graveyard to the battlefield. it gains haste. exile it at the beginning of the next end step or if it would leave the battlefield. unearth only as a sorcery

strategy_language: Unearth fits aristocrats — sac the creature before exile.

name: Unleash
rules_text_patterns: you may have this creature enter with a +1/+1 counter on it. it can't block as long as it has a +1/+1 counter on it
false_positive_notes: Unleash is a small +1/+1 modal at ETB; texture.
reason_language: trades blocking for a +1/+1 counter at ETB

name: Valiant
rules_text_patterns: whenever this creature becomes the target of a spell or ability you control for the first time each turn
false_positive_notes: Valiant is a Bloomburrow ability word for targeting your own creature; supports auras/heroic.

strategy_language: Valiant rewards self-targeting auras and equipment buffs.

name: Vanishing

false_positive_notes: Vanishing is a self-imposed time clock; supports proliferate plans loosely.

name: Wither
rules_text_patterns: this deals damage to creatures in the form of -1/-1 counters
false_positive_notes: Wither places -1/-1 counters via combat; supports infect-adjacent and -1/-1 shells.
reason_language: deals damage to creatures as -1/-1 counters
strategy_language: Wither pairs with proliferate and -1/-1 themes (Hapatra, Vraska's Conquistador).

name: Battalion
rules_text_patterns: whenever this creature and at least two other creatures attack
false_positive_notes: Battalion is Boros-flavored go-wide support. Limited card count keeps it from defining a deck on its own.
reason_language: rewards attacking with three or more creatures at once
strategy_language: Battalion needs reliable token production or board-wide haste to trigger consistently each combat.

name: Adamant
rules_text_patterns: if at least three mana of the same color was spent to cast this spell
false_positive_notes: Adamant is mostly a Standard-era bonus. In Commander it slots into mono-color decks but rarely becomes a build-around.
reason_language: grants a bonus when three mana of one color funds the spell
strategy_language: Adamant is essentially free in mono-color shells; deprioritize it in multicolor decks.

name: Channel
rules_text_patterns: discard this card:
channel —
false_positive_notes: Channel is two distinct things: original Kamigawa channel (discard for effect) and Neon Dynasty channel lands (cycle for utility). Both are flexible support, not a deck plan.
reason_language: lets a card double as a spell-from-hand effect
strategy_language: Channel cards play well in graveyard and madness shells where the discarded copy still matters.

name: Cohort
rules_text_patterns: tap this creature and tap an untapped ally you control
false_positive_notes: Cohort is narrow: it's an Ally-typal mechanic with very limited card count. Only relevant in Ally tribal builds.
reason_language: rewards tapping multiple Allies for repeatable activated effects
strategy_language: Cohort only works inside an Ally-tribal build; outside that shell it does nothing.

name: Constellation
rules_text_patterns: whenever an enchantment enters the battlefield under your control
false_positive_notes: Constellation defines enchantress decks. It is one of the strongest enchantment-payoff signals.
requires: ramp into multiple enchantments per turn
reason_language: triggers each time an enchantment lands, scaling with enchantment-heavy builds
strategy_language: Pair constellation with cheap auras and global enchantress draw; bestow lets one card trigger constellation twice.

name: Coven
rules_text_patterns: as long as you control three or more creatures with different powers
false_positive_notes: Coven needs three different power values, which is easy in token + creature mixes but requires deliberate building. Limited card count.
reason_language: rewards a board with three or more different-power creatures
strategy_language: Mix in 1/1 tokens, 2/2s, and larger creatures so coven turns on naturally instead of by accident.

name: Delirium
rules_text_patterns: as long as there are four or more card types among cards in your graveyard
false_positive_notes: Delirium is a real build-around but requires deliberate inclusion of all card types in the deck (especially artifact, enchantment, instant, sorcery, planeswalker, tribal/creature/land). Otherwise the trigger is unreliable.
reason_language: scales with four or more card types in the graveyard
strategy_language: Include cheap artifacts, enchantments, and a planeswalker or two so delirium turns on by turn 4-5 reliably.

name: Eminence
rules_text_patterns: as long as this is in the command zone or on the battlefield
false_positive_notes: Eminence only appears on a small handful of legendary creatures and is essentially commander-defining. Outside its specific commanders it doesn't matter.
reason_language: operates from the command zone, anchoring a strategy without needing to resolve the commander
strategy_language: Eminence rewards lean, focused builds — the commander shapes the deck even when uncast.

name: Ferocious
rules_text_patterns: if you control a creature with power 4 or greater
false_positive_notes: Ferocious is a small bonus tier; it's almost always free in big-creature decks but rarely the centerpiece.
reason_language: grants a bonus while a power 4+ creature is in play
strategy_language: Ferocious is automatic in midrange/stompy shells; don't build around it specifically.

name: Fateful Hour
rules_text_patterns: as long as you have 5 or less life
false_positive_notes: Fateful hour is a tiny mechanic (Dark Ascension only) — interesting but very low card count.
reason_language: rewards being on the brink of losing the game
strategy_language: Fateful hour only matters in self-damage or burn-yourself shells; otherwise treat it as flavor.

name: Formidable
rules_text_patterns: if creatures you control have total power 8 or greater
false_positive_notes: Formidable is almost always on already in big-creature decks; not a build-around by itself.
reason_language: rewards an already-developed board of large creatures
strategy_language: Formidable falls out of stompy decks naturally; don't bend the curve for it.

name: Hellbent
rules_text_patterns: as long as you have no cards in hand
false_positive_notes: Hellbent works inside discard/madness shells but requires deliberate hand emptying. Most decks won't reach an empty hand.
reason_language: scales while the hand is empty, rewarding aggressive resource use
strategy_language: Hellbent demands wheels or repeatable discard outlets; pair it with madness or graveyard payoffs to double-dip.

name: Heroic
rules_text_patterns: whenever you cast a spell that targets this creature
false_positive_notes: Heroic in Commander is niche but real — usually for voltron or single-target spell shells. Outside those, the trigger rarely fires meaningfully.
reason_language: triggers when a spell targets this creature, rewarding targeted-spell density
strategy_language: Heroic wants a high count of cheap spells that target the heroic creature — auras and protection spells work double duty.

name: Inspired
rules_text_patterns: whenever this creature becomes untapped
false_positive_notes: Inspired is a small mechanic (Born of the Gods). It's strong in untap-shenanigans builds but rarely enough to define a deck alone.
reason_language: triggers when this creature untaps, rewarding tap/untap loops
strategy_language: Inspired needs explicit tap-then-untap engines like Intruder Alarm or Seedborn Muse to be worth building around.

name: Join Forces
rules_text_patterns: starting with you, each player may pay any amount of mana
false_positive_notes: Join Forces is a tiny political mechanic (Commander 2011). Useful flavor but never a deck plan.
reason_language: invites the table to chip in mana for a group benefit
strategy_language: Join Forces shines in group-hug and politics decks where you're already trading favors with the table.

name: Kinship
rules_text_patterns: at the beginning of your upkeep, you may look at the top card of your library
false_positive_notes: Kinship is a Morningtide-only mechanic with very few cards. Only matters inside dedicated Changeling/tribal shells.
reason_language: rewards a tribal-dense library with passive upkeep value
strategy_language: Kinship pairs naturally with changelings and library-manipulation effects to guarantee hits.

name: Metalcraft
rules_text_patterns: as long as you control three or more artifacts
false_positive_notes: Metalcraft turns on easily in any artifact-leaning deck (mana rocks alone often hit it). Strong support but rarely the deck's spine on its own.
reason_language: scales while three or more artifacts are in play
strategy_language: Metalcraft is on by default in artifact decks; the question is whether the metalcraft payoffs themselves are strong enough to include.

name: Morbid
rules_text_patterns: if a creature died this turn
false_positive_notes: Morbid turns on naturally in aristocrats and creature-heavy decks. It's free value, but the morbid payoff cards must themselves be worth running.
reason_language: scales when a creature has died this turn, rewarding aristocrats lines
strategy_language: Morbid is automatic in sac-heavy shells; pair it with cheap sacrifice outlets for guaranteed activation.

name: Radiance
rules_text_patterns: target creature and each other creature that shares a color with it
false_positive_notes: Radiance is a Ravnica-block ability word with very few cards. Treat as flavor only.
reason_language: spreads an effect to all creatures sharing a color with the target
strategy_language: Radiance spells are situational removal/buff tools; build around board states where opposing colors line up.

name: Raid
rules_text_patterns: if you attacked this turn
false_positive_notes: Raid turns on automatically in aggressive shells, but the raid payoff cards must justify their slot. Rarely a deck spine.
reason_language: rewards attacking each turn
strategy_language: Raid is free in any deck that wants to attack; just make sure something can get through every turn.

name: Revolt
rules_text_patterns: if a permanent you controlled left the battlefield this turn
false_positive_notes: Revolt is broader than morbid (any permanent leaving counts) but still requires the payoff cards to earn their slot. Easy to enable with fetchlands or sac outlets.
reason_language: triggers when a permanent leaves your side this turn
strategy_language: Revolt enables off fetchlands, treasure sacrifices, and tokens dying — keep one cheap enabler in opening hands.

name: Spell Mastery
rules_text_patterns: if there are two or more instant and/or sorcery cards in your graveyard
false_positive_notes: Spell mastery is automatic in spellslinger and prowess shells. Strong support, not a deck plan.
reason_language: scales after two instants or sorceries hit the graveyard
strategy_language: Spell mastery comes online by turn 3-4 in any spell-based deck; deprioritize it in non-spellslinger builds.

name: Sweep
rules_text_patterns: return any number of basic lands you control to their owner's hand
false_positive_notes: Sweep is Saviors of Kamigawa-only and very small. Niche — only relevant with landfall or land-recursion engines.

strategy_language: Sweep cards work best with landfall payoffs that double-trigger when you replay the bounced lands.

name: Tempting Offer
rules_text_patterns: each other player may
false_positive_notes: Tempting Offer is a political mechanic — it's powerful in multiplayer but tied to a small card pool from the Commander product line.
reason_language: lets you take an effect that opponents can copy if they want
strategy_language: Tempting Offer cards work best when you'd benefit from acceleration even if everyone else also takes it.

name: Will of the Council
rules_text_patterns: starting with you, each player votes
false_positive_notes: Will of the Council is voting flavor — flexible political effects but a tiny card pool.
reason_language: puts an effect to a table-wide vote
strategy_language: Vote cards reward decks built to leverage table politics; expect the table to vote against your interests by default.

name: Council's Dilemma
rules_text_patterns: starting with you, each player votes
false_positive_notes: Council's Dilemma scales with vote totals (every vote matters). Same caveat as Will of the Council — small card pool.
reason_language: scales with votes cast around the table
strategy_language: Council's Dilemma scales with player count — strongest in 4-player pods.

name: Sagas
oracle_patterns: (as this saga enters and after your draw step, add a lore counter.

false_positive_notes: Sagas are a real build-around in enchantress and proliferate decks. Outside those shells they're individually strong cards but not a theme.

strategy_language: Sagas double-dip with proliferate (Tekuthal) and blink/recursion (Replicaeon, Yorion-style). Constellation and enchantress draw fire on each saga's ETB.

name: Vehicles

false_positive_notes: Vehicles can define a deck only when the commander rewards artifacts, vehicles specifically, or crew. A few high-power vehicles in a non-vehicle deck doesn't make it a vehicle theme.
reason_language: leverages crewable artifacts as efficient bodies and damage threats
strategy_language: Vehicle decks need cheap creatures to crew. Skysovereign-tier vehicles also slot into artifact decks just for raw power.

name: Battles
rules_text_patterns: battle — siege
false_positive_notes: Battles are a new card type with limited card count. Real synergy exists with attacking, proliferate (defense counters), and transform/DFC themes.
reason_language: uses battles as flippable threats that reward attacking with multiple creatures
strategy_language: Battles want a wide board so you can spare attackers to crack them. Proliferate is awkward (it removes defense counters too).

name: Backgrounds
rules_text_patterns: enchantment — background
false_positive_notes: Backgrounds matter only when paired with a 'Choose a Background' commander. Otherwise they're a small enchantment subset.
reason_language: anchors a permanent passive effect from the command zone
strategy_language: Backgrounds are partner-style commanders — pick one whose passive matches the deck plan, not just for value.

name: Dungeons
rules_text_patterns: enter the first room
false_positive_notes: Dungeons are useful in dedicated venture builds (Forgotten Realms, Battle for Baldur's Gate). Outside those shells the venture trigger rarely fires often enough to matter.
reason_language: ratchets through dungeon rooms to accumulate small advantages
strategy_language: Dungeons want repeatable venture triggers — blink, untap, and ETB engines all multiply the value.

name: Rooms
rules_text_patterns: enchantment — room
false_positive_notes: Rooms (Duskmourn) are a new enchantment subtype — modal split-enchantments that unlock in halves. Real synergy with enchantress and constellation.
reason_language: delivers two enchantment effects from one card via the unlock structure
strategy_language: Rooms double-dip with enchantress draw — unlocking the second half is treated as casting a new enchantment for some triggers.

name: Mounts
oracle_patterns: as long as this creature is saddled

false_positive_notes: Mounts (Outlaws of Thunder Junction) are similar to vehicles but they're already creatures. Theme requires a saddle/mount commander or rider payoff.
reason_language: uses mounts as durable threats activated by a saddler each combat
strategy_language: Mount decks need cheap creatures to saddle each turn. Unlike vehicles, mounts already attack and block uncrewed.

name: Auras
rules_text_patterns: enchantment — aura
false_positive_notes: Auras-matter is a real theme (voltron, enchantress, bestow). A handful of auras alone in a deck does not make it an aura deck.
requires: protection for the creature being enchanted
reason_language: stacks auras on a single creature for cumulative power and triggers
strategy_language: Aura decks live or die on protection (totem armor, hexproof, recursion). Lose the creature without protection and the deck collapses.

name: Equipment
rules_text_patterns: artifact — equipment
false_positive_notes: Equipment-matter requires the commander or several payoffs to reward equip activations or equipped-creature value. Casual swords in a deck do not make it equipment.
reason_language: stacks equipment on a creature to convert artifact density into combat damage
strategy_language: Equipment voltron needs Sigarda's Aid, Puresteel Paladin, or similar engines to bypass the equip-cost tax.

name: Classes
rules_text_patterns: enchantment — class
false_positive_notes: Classes (AFR) are leveled enchantments. They support enchantress decks but the card pool is small.
reason_language: delivers staged enchantment value through level-up upgrades
strategy_language: Classes are mana sinks; only run them when the enchantress payoff or first-level effect alone earns its slot.

name: Eerie
rules_text_patterns: whenever an enchantment you control enters and whenever you fully unlock a room
false_positive_notes: Eerie (Duskmourn) is a constellation-adjacent ability word that also triggers on Room unlocks. Strong in enchantress shells with rooms; otherwise narrow.
reason_language: triggers on enchantment ETBs and Room unlocks alike
strategy_language: Eerie cards stack with constellation — both fire from the same enchantment ETBs.

name: Survival
rules_text_patterns: at the beginning of the second main phase, if this creature is tapped
false_positive_notes: Survival (Bloomburrow) checks if the creature is tapped on second main — pairs with vigilance, tap outlets, and attack-friendly shells.
reason_language: rewards a tapped creature surviving past combat each turn
strategy_language: Survival pairs cleanly with vigilance — attack and still trigger. Crew/saddle/tap activations also work.

name: Gift
rules_text_patterns: you may promise an opponent
false_positive_notes: Gift (Outlaws of Thunder Junction) promises an opponent a benefit in exchange for a bigger one for you. Real but small card pool; political flavor.
reason_language: trades a small benefit to an opponent for a larger upgrade on your spell
strategy_language: Pick the gift target who's least threatening — usually a player already behind so the gift means little.

name: Spree
rules_text_patterns: choose one or more additional costs
false_positive_notes: Spree (Outlaws of Thunder Junction) is modal additional costs — flexible but each card stands alone. Not a build-around.
reason_language: stacks optional cost-modes for situational power
strategy_language: Spree cards scale with mana — they shine in mana-rich late-game shells.

name: Suspect
rules_text_patterns: a suspected creature has menace and can't block
false_positive_notes: Suspect (Murders at Karlov Manor) gives menace at the cost of blocking. Aggressive support, often used on your own creatures or as soft removal.
reason_language: grants menace to push damage through
strategy_language: Suspect on opponents' creatures is soft removal in a tight board; suspect on your own creatures is an evasion engine.

name: Collect Evidence
rules_text_patterns: exile any number of cards with total mana value
false_positive_notes: Collect evidence (Murders at Karlov Manor) requires graveyard density. Real synergy with self-mill, but the payoff card pool is limited.
reason_language: exiles graveyard cards as fuel for scaling effects
strategy_language: Collect evidence competes with delve, escape, and reanimation for graveyard real estate. Build deep mill or accept overlap.

name: Cases
oracle_patterns: as long as this case is solved
rules_text_patterns: enchantment — case
false_positive_notes: Cases (MKM) are conditional enchantments — they reward fulfilling a clue (attack with creatures, mill cards, etc). Useful in enchantress shells; small card pool.
reason_language: delivers a staged enchantment that unlocks a stronger effect once its condition is met
strategy_language: Pick cases whose condition is already part of the deck plan — don't bend the deck to solve a case.

name: Impending
rules_text_patterns: if this spell's impending cost was paid
false_positive_notes: Impending (Tarkir: Dragonstorm) is a discount-now-pay-later mechanic with time counters. Pairs with proliferate (counter manipulation) and time-counter shells.
reason_language: lets a spell come down cheap with delayed full effect via time counters
strategy_language: Impending pairs with proliferate to remove time counters faster, and with cards that care about time counters.

name: Reanimator
oracle_patterns: return target creature card from your graveyard to the battlefield
put target creature card from a graveyard onto the battlefield
return target creature from your graveyard to the battlefield
false_positive_notes: Reanimation effects are common in many decks. The reanimator THEME requires both setup (mill/discard) and big targets to cheat in. A few graveyard returns alone is graveyard value, not reanimator.
scryfall_query_terms: o:"return target creature card from your graveyard"
reason_language: cheats expensive creatures into play from the graveyard
strategy_language: Reanimator wants both halves: ways to put fatties in the bin (Entomb, Buried Alive, mill) and reanimation spells. Without the setup, the deck stalls.

name: Lifegain
oracle_patterns: if you gained life this turn
false_positive_notes: Lifegain is a deck only when there's a payoff for gaining life — Soul Sisters triggers, drain effects, lifegain-counter doublers. Incidental lifegain (lifelink, fetch pain) is texture, not theme.
reason_language: converts repeated lifegain into card draw, counters, drain, or scaling threats
strategy_language: Lifegain matters only with payoffs. Pair it with Aetherflux Reservoir, Aristocrats drains, or counter scaling — gaining life alone wins nothing.

name: Enter the Battlefield
oracle_patterns: whenever a creature you control enters
false_positive_notes: Almost every creature has an ETB. The ETB THEME means the deck explicitly multiplies them (Panharmonicon, blink, copying ETB creatures). A pile of ETB creatures alone is texture.
reason_language: scales by repeating or doubling enter-the-battlefield triggers
strategy_language: ETB decks live on Panharmonicon-style doublers and reusable blink (Brago, Roon, Conjurer's Closet). Without those, ETBs are one-shots.

name: Blink
oracle_patterns: exile target creature, then return it to the battlefield
exile target creature you control, then return it to the battlefield
exile target permanent you control, then return it to the battlefield
false_positive_notes: Blink/Flicker is the engine that powers ETB decks. Without ETB-rich creatures, blinking does nothing useful. A few flicker spells alone don't make a blink deck.
reason_language: repeats enter-the-battlefield value through exile-and-return loops
strategy_language: Blink decks need both halves: cheap repeatable blink (Soulherder, Brago) and ETBs worth abusing (Mulldrifter, Reflector Mage).

name: Spellslinger
oracle_patterns: whenever you cast an instant or sorcery
whenever you cast a noncreature spell
false_positive_notes: Spellslinger means instants/sorceries are the win condition or main fuel. A control deck with counterspells is not automatically spellslinger — it needs payoffs (prowess, magecraft, storm count, copy).
scryfall_query_terms: o:"whenever you cast an instant or sorcery"
reason_language: scales with each instant or sorcery cast, turning a spell pile into a payoff engine
strategy_language: Spellslinger needs ~30+ instants/sorceries plus reliable draw. Include creatures only as payoffs (Young Pyromancer, Murmuring Mystic) or utility.

name: Voltron
false_positive_notes: Voltron is a strategy, not a keyword. Detection requires: commander designed to be buffed, dense auras/equipment, and protection. A deck with one or two swords is not voltron.
reason_language: stacks buffs on a single threat to deliver lethal commander damage
strategy_language: Voltron is fragile to spot removal — prioritize Lightning Greaves, Swiftfoot Boots, and recursion. Evasion (trample, flying, unblockable) is non-negotiable.

name: Group Hug
false_positive_notes: Group hug is a strategy where you accelerate the entire table. Detection is the table-wide effect cluster — a single Howling Mine doesn't make a deck group hug.
requires: win condition (often pillowfort or hidden combo)
reason_language: accelerates the whole table to dictate pace and earn goodwill
strategy_language: Group hug usually needs a private win condition (Approach of the Second Sun, Maze's End) so the help you give doesn't kill you first.

name: Stax
false_positive_notes: Stax means systematic resource denial via permanents (Winter Orb, Smokestack, Tangle Wire). One Rhystic Study or one Cursed Totem is not stax.
reason_language: throttles the entire table's resources while you build asymmetric advantage
strategy_language: Stax is socially difficult and slow. Build in asymmetry (artifact mana, untap effects, hexproof commander) and a quick win behind the lock.

name: Aristocrats
oracle_patterns: whenever a creature you control dies
false_positive_notes: Aristocrats requires the trifecta: sacrifice outlets, sac fodder (tokens, recursive creatures), and death payoffs (drain, draw, counters). Two of three is incomplete.
scryfall_query_terms: o:"whenever a creature you control dies"
reason_language: loops creatures through sacrifice and recursion to bleed the table on each death
strategy_language: Aristocrats wants free sac outlets (Viscera Seer, Carrion Feeder), recurring fodder (Reassembling Skeleton, Bitterblossom), and drains (Blood Artist, Zulaport Cutthroat).

name: Typal
false_positive_notes: Typal is detected by creature-type density and lord effects. A scattering of Goblins isn't a Goblin deck — needs payoffs and 25+ tribal creatures.
requires: high tribal density (25+ creatures of type)
reason_language: stacks anthems and synergies across a creature-type-focused board
strategy_language: Typal decks need both density and payoffs. Changelings and tribal lands (Cavern of Souls, Unclaimed Territory) reduce risk.

name: Top of Library
oracle_patterns: the top card of your library
play the top card of your library
false_positive_notes: Scry alone isn't a top-of-library theme. The theme requires playing from the top (Future Sight, Vivien's Arkbow), top-deck payoffs (Bolas's Citadel, Magus of the Future), or cascade-style triggers.
scryfall_query_terms: o:"play the top card of your library"
reason_language: leverages the top of the library as a second hand or repeated trigger source
strategy_language: Top-of-library decks need both manipulation (Top, Brainstorm, Scroll Rack) and payoffs that play from the top. Without manipulation, you flood out.

name: Land Animation
oracle_patterns: target land you control becomes a creature
until end of turn, target land you control becomes
false_positive_notes: Land animation is part of Lands.dec strategies (Titania, Lord Windgrace) but a few manlands alone don't make it the theme. Real shells run Nissa Awakener, Living Lands, and protection.
scryfall_query_terms: o:"target land you control becomes a creature"
reason_language: turns lands into resilient threats while preserving mana production
strategy_language: Land-based decks pair animation with indestructibility (Nine Lives, Avacyn, the Purifier) so wraths don't undo the entire mana base.

name: Tutors
oracle_patterns: search your library for a card
false_positive_notes: Tutors are support, not a theme. They power combo and consistency decks but don't define a strategy by themselves. Detection should treat them as a power-level signal, not a deck plan.
requires: specific cards worth tutoring (combo pieces, silver bullets)
reason_language: fetches the right answer or combo piece on demand
strategy_language: Heavy tutoring linearizes games — use sparingly in casual pods. Pair tutors with toolbox creatures (Birthing Pod, Survival of the Fittest) for replay value.

name: Spell Copy
oracle_patterns: copy target instant or sorcery spell
false_positive_notes: Spell copy is real engine territory in Izzet/Jeskai spellslinger decks. A single Reverberate isn't a copy theme — needs Twincast effects, Veyran-style commanders, and spell density.
reason_language: doubles instants and sorceries to scale damage, draw, or token output
strategy_language: Copy themes pair with token-generating spells (Krenko, Mob Boss; Empty the Warrens) and burn for scaling damage.

name: Doubling Effects
false_positive_notes: Doublers (Doubling Season, Parallel Lives, Anointed Procession, Mondrak) define token decks but are also generic value cards. The theme requires multiple doublers stacked AND something to double.
reason_language: multiplies tokens, counters, or trigger output for exponential scaling
strategy_language: Doublers stack multiplicatively — three doublers turn one token into eight. Build redundancy and tutors for them.

name: Extra Combats
false_positive_notes: Extra-combat themes pair with attack triggers, combat-damage payoffs, and Voltron. A single Aggravated Assault doesn't make a deck extra-combats.
reason_language: compounds attack-trigger value and combat damage with each extra combat
strategy_language: Extra combats want haste, evasion, and triggers that fire per-attack. Aggravated Assault + mana doublers can chain into infinite combats.

name: Extra Turns
oracle_patterns: take an extra turn after this one
take another turn after this one
false_positive_notes: Extra-turn decks are a real archetype (Narset, Nekusar-style chain combos). A single Time Walk effect in a deck is not the theme — needs Beacon of Tomorrows, recursion, or commander-driven turn loops.
scryfall_query_terms: o:"take an extra turn after this one"
requires: win condition during the extra turn
reason_language: stacks turns to leverage commander or attack triggers without table interaction
strategy_language: Extra-turn shells need a closer (commander damage, mill, infect) to convert turns into a win. Without it, the deck stalls behind Time Walks with no kill.
